trajectory_summary: The call opened flat, briefly spiked when Kellina volunteered strong enthusiasm signals (trees removed, new roof, full sun), and then drained as bill collection stalled and the rep punted to an unstructured async follow-up. No appointment was booked. The call ends with Kellina saying 'maybe I'll look' — a polite non-commitment that has a high probability of going cold.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as 'unknown.' Based on the transcript, no appointment was booked and no locked callback time was established. The outcome should be tagged as 'no_appointment / soft_follow_up_pending' — and the follow-up is structurally fragile: no date, no time, no confirmation, no button-up elements completed.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: Another thing you can do is, just give Pacific Power a call. And they can send you a PDF to your email. Mhmm. And then, if you just wanna forward that over to my email, I can text you my email.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The rep had a customer with a physical bill in hand at 04:18 and never attempted verbal readback — the codex's explicit pivot for this exact situation. Instead he built a three-step async path (call utility → get PDF → email to rep) that ended with no appointment, no locked callback time, and a 'maybe I'll look' from Kellina. This is the single move that determined the call's outcome. If the rep pivots to verbal readback at 04:18, he gets the kWh data, books the appointment, and this is a different call. Manager should walk through the codex's bill_collection.alternative_paths.elderly_low_tech_protocol with the rep and role-play the pivot moment.

key_moments.quote: No. We cut a lot of them. So the the roof is, in full sun. Okay? The the roof is new, and it's full sun.
key_moments.stewart_read: Kellina volunteers three qualifying upgrades in one breath: trees removed (improved sun exposure), roof new (no re-roof barrier), full sun (optimal install conditions). This is the highest-value moment in the call. The rep's response was to confirm the roof material and move on — the signal was heard but not anchored.

key_moments.quote: And then Kellina, please please tell me Kellina. It's not Michelina. Okay?
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep mispronounced the customer's name on the first substantive exchange. Kellina corrected him with audible emphasis ('please please'). The rep's recovery at 00:37 ('Oh, okay. Okay. Michelina. Perfect. Thank you.') was adequate — he acknowledged and moved on without dwelling. The repair was functional but not warm.

key_moments.quote: I have you done as a property owner. Is there anyone else on the title of the home with you? Or is it Yeah.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep asked the title question correctly and Kellina confirmed her wife is on the title. The rep's response was 'Okay. No problem. We'll go ahead and put that down here.' — he noted it and moved on. There is no evidence in the transcript that the rep ensured the wife would be included in the appointment. Since no appointment was booked, this didn't become a structural vulnerability in the booking itself — but the rep never flagged the co-decision-maker inclusion requirement.

key_moments.quote: Only requirement is that you have a credit score above 6 170.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep stated the credit threshold as '6 170' — likely a garbled '670' or possibly '617.' This is the third data point across the corpus where the stated threshold doesn't cleanly match the codex's documented 670 (SESSION18_fcbf33c1 stated 650; this call states something ambiguous). The codex's schema_drift_detection directive requires flagging rep-stated numbers that don't match the schema.
